12. Foundation Models (Omar Khattab)

12.1. Pretraining (Next word predicton)

Ideas for pretraining include RL, SL, and SSL.

  1. Reinforcement learning (action = generating a token, reward = whether humans like similar prompts). Too sparse, used in post-training instead

    1. Produce output for a prompt
    2. Reward model (separate architecture) scores it
    3. RL algorithm adjusts weights via proximal policy optimization

  2. Supervised learning

    • Problem with finding good questions and answers.

      • SQuAD (2016) dataset: 100,000 human-crafted questions touching on a measly 500 Wikipedia pages. E.g. “Which NFL team represented the AFC at Super Bowl 50”
      • HotPotQA (2018): 100,000 questions about pairs of Wikipedia pages. These questions are boring though
      • MS Marco (2016): 1,000,000 Bing queries. A lot of queries are short.
      • Natural Questions (2019): 100,000 Google queries.
    • This is not scalable and doesn’t adapt to new information.

  3. Self-supervised learning

    • Powerful training task is language modeling: Train next-token / autocomplete machine and do this on the entire web. Some examples of how this was done:

      • 20 years ago google trained 300 billion parameter n-gram model (parameter = n-gram, counting a sequence of words). n-gram uses markov assumption: . If the model has never seen that -gram context the probability is undefined so it recurses on a smaller n-grams (back-off) or with interpolation (mixing probabilities at those smaller levels)

      • -nearest neighbors:

        1. Choose a context length L
        2. For every token, store pair (x_t-L, …, x_t-1) → (x_t) in a searchable index with a similarity metric
        3. At inference time, take the k nearest neighbors, collect their next tokens and predict via a majority/distance-weighted vote
      • Why not BERT style: (1) you throw away learning signal from the words you didn’t mask. (2) Not clear how you can use this for generative modelling. Just add masked tokens to the right?

12.2. LLMs

  • Motivation for LLMs: Language provides a natural domain for the study of artificial in- telligence, as the vast majority of reasoning tasks can be efficiently expressed and evaluated in language, and the world’s text provides a wealth of data for unsupervised learning via generative modeling.

  • Design choices: people are really focused on scaling stuff up so they adhere to past priors. Here’s an example

    • Architecture: Decoder-only transformer with casual mask
    • Context window:   100,000 words
    • Pre-training: Make LLMs good next word predictors
    • Post-training: Teach LLMs to be instruction following assistances and be good at math, coding, tools, etc.
  • Observation: Including code in the pretraining data seems to improve reasoning.

  • Ideas for tokenization:

    1. Use whitespace to determine words: ❌️ Bad because mispellings and out of vocabulary
    2. Characters: too long. ❌️ Inductive bias too weak
    3. Subword tokens ✔️
    • Tokenization refers to converting text into token IDs (what way we can interpret logits). Then we run a token embedde
  • Inductive biases: it’s hard to design them so we let the model learn them

  • How to train the web: crawl pages

    • Most pages are low quality: misinformation/toxic

    • Ideas

      1. Oversample informative sites: Wikipedia, arXiv, Github, stack exchange, reddit (changed API in 2023 to prevent free scraping)
      2. Filter based on known heuristics like upvotes or Google search PageRank
      3. Ask LLM to assess pages, distill and run at scale

  • Monitoring training:

    • Monitor loss over time and watch out for spikes (gradients explode, hard to be stable at scale)

    • Example problem: Repetitive text in a batch gives redundant gradients pointing in the same direction, causing spikes in loss curve

  • How to train? Data? Architecture? Hyperparams?

    • Cannot try a bunch of large models
    • Tuning hyperparameters at small scale doesn’t transfer easily nor how to scale them
    • Scaling laws: train and tune many small models. HPO for each of the scales and then fit global scaling laws and then retune at the new scale.
    • Good ideas are often simple but need to be aggressively scaled

12.3. Evaluation

  • “The Bitter lesson” by Rich sutton: more compute with simple methods seem to beat specialized methods

  • Loss on pre-training validation set (avg cross entropy loss across tokens; recall pretraining is next-word prediction

    • Perplexity is : On average, the model is as uncertain as selecting between PPL equally likely options

  • Problems with benchmarks

    • Contamination: Make sure benchmarks aren’t in your training data
    • Hill climbing: Researchers repeatedly evaluate and tune on benchmarks (model implicitly sees the data)
    • Emergence: Some capabilities are easier to generate then evaluate, e.g. creating poetry.

12.4. Scaling Laws

  • Given limited compute, how do we allocate it among model size, dataset size, and training duration?
  • Brute force is too slow, and hyperparameters don’t transfer well with small-scale tuning. We’ll use scaling laws.

  • For a fixed architecture, the test loss L scales as a power law of a resource (data, parameter, compute):

    (24)

    where α is empirically and is irreducible loss.

  • Kaplan 2020:

    • follows over many order of magnitude.
    • Loss is more sensitive to model size than data size.
    • Given a compute budget C, we should have , ( is num parameters and is dataset size) and you should increase faster than as grows.
    • The power law implies: Don’t train to convergence. Train large model shorters. This is not true anymore.
    • [TODO] Phenomenological reasoning based on bias variance tradeoff and statistics

  • Chinchilla (Deepmind 2022):

    • Same scaling law but challenged Kaplan’s compute allocation. Optimal scaling when parameters and data scale equally: . Thus
    • Under Chinchilla scaling, models trained longer on more data outperform overly large, undertrained ones. Shift towards overtraining!

  • Scaling laws have diminishing returns. Also lowering perplexity is not necessarily a good measure of model capability if we are in the threshold regime (emergence; see next section) rather than smooth regime (small loss reductions cause quantative improvements).

    • Future gains come from post-training, data quality, and inference-time reasoning (tool use, retrieval, and planning)
    • Scale is necessary but not sufficient.

12.5. Emergent Behaviors

  • Emergence is defined as qualitative capability appearing only once a model exceeds a certain scale threshold.

    • Examples of such behavior include few-shot learning, chain-of-thought reasoning, and tool use and self-consistency prompting.
    • Smaller models lack the latent structure to be able to do these when prompting.
    • For LLMs, few shot learning is defined as when you give the models examples embedded in the prompt. E.g.
    Translate English to French:
    dog → chien
    cat → chat
    house → maison
    car → ?

    Zero-shot is with no examples.

12.6. [REVIEW] Post Training (SFT, RL)

Recall pre-training is next-token prediction on huge, messy web data and high-quality domain specific material which yields strong language priors and world knowledge. See the pretraining recipe (Figure 61). Scaling alone gives diminishing returns. Small curated datasets can steer pretrained models efficiently toward human-preferred outputs.

SFT narrows the search space so RL is feasible. Standard SFT uses teacher forcing

12.6.1. Supervised Fine-tuning

  • We’ll use next-token prediction on human-authored Q/A and open-assistant style conversations.

  • Failure modes:

    • Hallucination
    • Over-fitting domains: e.g. overtraining on math yields fragile reasoning.
    • Problems with multiple valid answers are not well described by a single label.
    • Hard to teach reasoning

12.6.2. Reinforcement Learning for LLMs

  • Read Section 1.4 for exposition on RL.
  • Prompt plays the role of the state. Completion is the model’s action.

  • Train a reward network from human data and optimize expected reward while keeping model close to initial policy . The true objective function is the KL-regularized expected reward objective.

    • We estimate above using the PPO reward function with critic.
    • Critic lets us compute advantages token-by-token making the learning signal smoother instead of only using the scalar reward at the end of the generating sequence. Critic tries to estimate reward given prompt plus prefix up to . The advantage for each prefix is .
    • Critic and policy can be two heads of a transformer based on trajectory data (see learning loop below).

    • Learning loop:

      1. Sample prompt, generate response and score them.
      2. Estimate advantages using old critic network.
      3. Compute PPO loss and update for policy and update critic network.
  • A simpler RL method is called rejection fine-tuning. For a given prompt: sample responses, and keep those with reward above a threshold. Retrain on accepted using standard supervised fine tuning (e.g. teacher forcing).

  • Feedbacks example

    • RLHF: Annotators judge responses pointwise or pairwise.
    • RLAIF: replace human raters with LLM-based evaluators, but this might propagate evaluator bias.
    • RLVR: Verifiable reward; use deterministic grading functions for objectively checkable tasks. Yields rapid progress in math/coding.

12.6.3. Open Questions

  • Can RL alone bootstrap reasoning without rich pretraining priors? (Evidence: not yet)
  • How to define rewards that encourage process reasoning rather than superficial text patterns?
  • Should we preserve proximity to (safety, generalization) or allow divergence (creativity, reasoning depth).
